rows.v: A shared, tamper-resistant digital ledger that records transactions in linked 'blocks' across many computers. The foundational technology behind cryptocurrencies.
Every transaction was permanently recorded on the blockchain for anyone to verify.
A compound of 'block' (a batch of transactions) + 'chain' (cryptographically linked in sequence). Each block references the last, making the history extremely hard to alter.
Describes a system run by many independent participants rather than a single central authority like a bank or government. The core ideal of crypto.
Because the network was decentralized, no single company or government could shut it down.
From 'de-' (removal of) + 'central'. The aspiration to remove central gatekeepers — spreading control across a distributed network — is crypto's founding philosophy.
The process of validating transactions and creating new coins by solving complex computations. Miners are rewarded with cryptocurrency for securing the network.
His powerful rig spent all day mining, racing other computers to validate the next block.
Likened to mining gold — expending effort (here, computing power) to extract new currency. The metaphor captures both the reward and the resource cost.
A computer that participates in a blockchain network, storing a copy of the ledger and helping validate and relay transactions. The network is built of nodes.
Running a full node, she kept her own complete copy of the blockchain to verify everything independently.
From the general sense of a 'node' as a connection point in a network. Each participating computer is a node, and their distribution is what makes the system resilient.
The mechanism by which a decentralized network agrees on which transactions are valid, without a central authority. How strangers' computers reach agreement.
The network reached consensus on the valid chain, rejecting the fraudulent transactions.
From 'consensus' (general agreement). The hard problem crypto solves: getting thousands of distrustful, anonymous computers to agree on a single shared truth.
Two ways to reach consensus: 'Proof of Work' requires solving hard computations (energy-intensive); 'Proof of Stake' requires locking up coins as collateral (energy-light).
The network switched from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake, slashing its energy use dramatically.
Descriptive: you prove your stake in the network either through 'work' (computation) or a 'stake' (deposited coins). The two dominant approaches to securing a blockchain.
A distributed ledger is a database of transactions copied and synchronized across many locations. Unlike a bank's private books, it's shared and publicly verifiable.
The distributed ledger meant thousands of identical copies existed, with no single point of failure.
From the accounting 'ledger' (a record book). 'Distributed' because the book is copied across the whole network rather than held privately by one institution.
Self-executing code on a blockchain that automatically carries out an agreement when conditions are met — no middleman needed. The engine of much of crypto.
The smart contract automatically released the funds the instant the goods were confirmed delivered.
Coined by Nick Szabo in the 1990s. 'Smart' because it enforces itself, and 'contract' because it codifies an agreement — executed by code rather than courts.
The first and largest cryptocurrency, created in 2009 as decentralized digital money. Often called 'digital gold' and used as a store of value.
He bought a fraction of a Bitcoin as a long-term bet on digital money.
Created by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto in 2009; the name simply joins 'bit' (a unit of digital data) with 'coin' (money). The original cryptocurrency.
Any cryptocurrency other than Bitcoin — short for 'alternative coin.' Thousands exist, from major platforms to obscure speculative tokens.
After Bitcoin, she diversified into a handful of promising altcoins.
A compound of 'alternative' + 'coin' — everything that isn't Bitcoin. The term captures the vast, varied field of coins that followed the original.
A digital asset created on top of an existing blockchain, often representing anything from currency to ownership to access rights. Distinct from a blockchain's native coin.
The project issued its own token to give holders voting rights in the platform.
From 'token' (a symbol or representation of value). Unlike a blockchain's native coin, tokens are built atop existing chains and can represent almost anything.
A cryptocurrency designed to hold a steady value, usually pegged to a currency like the US dollar. A refuge from crypto's wild price swings.
She parked her profits in a stablecoin to escape volatility without cashing out entirely.
A compound emphasizing 'stable' value — engineered to avoid the volatility of typical crypto by pegging to an external asset like the dollar.
Non-Fungible Token — a unique digital asset whose ownership is recorded on a blockchain, often representing art, collectibles, or media. Each one is one-of-a-kind.
He bought an NFT of the digital artwork, owning a verifiable one-of-a-kind original.
'Non-fungible' means non-interchangeable — unlike a dollar or a Bitcoin, each NFT is unique. The acronym names a token proving ownership of a singular item.
The fee paid to process a transaction or run a smart contract on a blockchain. Gas fees rise and fall with network demand, sometimes dramatically.
The transaction stalled until he paid higher gas fees to prioritize it.
Metaphorically the 'fuel' that powers transactions — like gas in a car. The busier the network, the more 'gas' you must pay to get your transaction processed.
A scheduled event where the reward for mining new Bitcoin is cut in half, roughly every four years. It slows new supply and is closely watched by investors.
The market buzzed with speculation ahead of the next halving and its effect on supply.
Descriptive: the mining reward is 'halved'. Built into Bitcoin's code to gradually reduce new supply, each halving is a major, anticipated event.
Decentralized Finance — financial services (lending, trading, earning interest) built on blockchains without banks or brokers. Finance run by code, open to anyone.
Through DeFi, she lent out her crypto and earned interest with no bank involved.
A compound of 'decentralized' + 'finance'. The movement to rebuild financial services — loans, exchanges, interest — on open blockchains instead of institutions.
Decentralized Exchange — a platform for trading crypto directly between users via smart contracts, without a central company holding your funds.
He swapped tokens on a DEX, trading peer-to-peer without trusting any company with his coins.
An acronym for 'decentralized exchange'. Unlike a traditional exchange, a DEX never takes custody of your funds — trades happen directly via code.
A crowdsourced pile of funds locked in a smart contract that enables trading on decentralized exchanges. Users supply the pool and earn fees in return.
She added her tokens to a liquidity pool, earning a share of the trading fees.
Descriptive: a 'pool' of funds providing 'liquidity' (available capital) for trades. It replaces the traditional buyer-meets-seller model with a shared communal reserve.
Locking up cryptocurrency to help secure a Proof-of-Stake network, earning rewards in return. A way to earn passive income on holdings.
By staking his coins, he earned steady rewards while helping validate the network.
From 'stake' (to put up as collateral). You commit ('stake') your coins to support the network's security, and the protocol rewards you for it.
Actively moving crypto between DeFi protocols to chase the highest returns. A complex, high-risk pursuit of maximum yield.
Yield farming across protocols chased double-digit returns, but the risks were just as high.
An agricultural metaphor: 'farming' the highest 'yield' (return) by cultivating funds across protocols. It captures the active, return-maximizing hustle of DeFi.
Market capitalization — a cryptocurrency's total value, calculated as price times circulating supply. The standard measure of a coin's overall size.
Despite a low price per coin, its massive supply gave it a huge market cap.
Borrowed from stock markets. A coin's cheap unit price can be misleading — market cap (price × supply) reveals its true total size relative to others.
The difference between a trade's expected price and the price it actually executes at, caused by price movement or low liquidity. The cost of a moving market.
The large order suffered heavy slippage, filling at a worse price than he expected.
From 'slip' — the price 'slips' between order and execution. Common in volatile or thin markets, where a big trade moves the price against itself.
Software or hardware that stores the keys controlling your cryptocurrency and lets you send and receive it. Your gateway to your crypto — though the coins live on the blockchain.
She transferred the coins to her own wallet, taking direct control of her funds.
From the everyday 'wallet'. Slightly misleading — it holds the cryptographic keys that control your coins, not the coins themselves, which live on the chain.
A secret cryptographic code that proves ownership and authorizes spending of your crypto. Whoever holds the private key controls the funds — guard it absolutely.
He kept his private key offline and secret, knowing anyone who had it could take everything.
Descriptive: a 'private', secret 'key' that unlocks control of your funds. The cardinal rule of crypto — 'not your keys, not your coins' — flows from this.
A list of (usually 12 or 24) random words that can restore access to a crypto wallet. The master backup — and a catastrophic loss if it falls into the wrong hands.
She wrote her seed phrase on paper and locked it away, never storing it online.
Also called a 'recovery phrase'. The 'seed' from which all your wallet's keys are generated — a human-readable master backup that must be protected at all costs.
Keeping crypto keys completely offline, on a device or paper disconnected from the internet, for maximum security against hackers. The opposite of a 'hot' (online) wallet.
For his long-term holdings, he used cold storage, keeping the keys entirely offline.
Descriptive: storage kept 'cold' (offline), versus a 'hot' wallet connected to the internet. Disconnecting from the net removes the biggest avenue for theft.
A blockchain address is a unique string of characters identifying where crypto can be sent, like an account number. Public and shareable, unlike the private key.
She shared her wallet address so the payment could be sent to her.
Like an account number or email for receiving funds. The address is derived from your keys and is safe to share publicly — it only lets others send you crypto.
Decentralized Application — software that runs on a blockchain via smart contracts rather than on a company's central servers. The apps of the Web3 world.
He used a dApp to trade and lend, all running on the blockchain instead of a company's servers.
A compound of 'decentralized' + 'app'. Pronounced 'dapp', these applications run on a blockchain's distributed network rather than corporate servers.
A secondary network built on top of a main blockchain (Layer 1) to make transactions faster and cheaper, easing congestion on the base chain.
By moving to a Layer 2, his transactions became nearly instant and cost a fraction of the fees.
Descriptive: a second 'layer' atop the base blockchain. By handling transactions off the main chain, Layer 2s tackle the speed and cost limits of popular networks.
A famous misspelling of 'hold' that became a mantra for keeping your crypto through volatility rather than selling. Often backronymed as 'Hold On for Dear Life.'
Through the brutal crash, she chose to HODL rather than panic-sell.
Born from a drunken, typo-ridden 2013 forum post titled 'I AM HODLING'. The misspelling went viral and became crypto's rallying cry for steadfast holding.
'FOMO' (Fear Of Missing Out) drives people to buy in a rising frenzy; 'FUD' (Fear, Uncertainty, Doubt) spreads negativity to drive prices down. Two emotional forces.
He bought the top out of FOMO, then panic-sold the bottom because of FUD.
Acronyms for the twin emotions ruling crypto markets. FOMO is greed-driven buying; FUD is fear-driven selling, often spread deliberately to manipulate sentiment.
A person or entity holding a very large amount of cryptocurrency, big enough to move the market with a single trade. Smaller traders watch whales closely.
When a whale dumped thousands of coins, the price plunged in minutes.
From the casino term for a high-roller, and the image of an enormous creature making waves. A whale's massive holdings let it ripple — or crash — the whole market.
A scam where developers hype a project, attract investment, then suddenly abandon it and vanish with the money. A notorious crypto fraud.
The project turned out to be a rug pull — the founders disappeared overnight with everyone's funds.
From 'pulling the rug out' from under someone. The creators yank away the project (and the money), leaving investors to come crashing down.
An exclamation expressing hope or hype that a coin's price will skyrocket. 'We're going to the moon!' is the optimist's battle cry.
As the price surged, the chat filled with rocket emojis and cries of 'to the moon!'
A vivid metaphor for a price rocketing skyward, often paired with rocket emojis. The hopeful, hype-fueled rallying cry of crypto bulls everywhere.
Slang for holding an investment with unshakable conviction through extreme volatility, refusing to sell. The opposite is 'paper hands' — selling at the first scare.
She had diamond hands, gripping her coins through every crash without flinching.
A metaphor: hands strong as 'diamond' that won't drop the asset under pressure. Popularized in meme-stock and crypto culture as a badge of steadfast resolve.
